positioning: Educational scenario model, not a claimed Waymo or Tesla P&L or investment forecast.

waymo_like.label: Waymo-like evidence case
waymo_like.description: Premium vehicle, multi-sensor, ODD-led full-stack robotaxi architecture with the strongest available operational evidence.
waymo_like.evidence_posture: Evidence-led scenario with several undisclosed cost lines.

cybercab.label: Cybercab claim-based case
cybercab.description: Purpose-built, camera-led, map-light and manufacturing-led architecture with potentially lower vehicle and launch cost but substantially wider uncertainty.
cybercab.evidence_posture: Claim-based architecture. Low-end values are sensitivity endpoints, not observed economics.

waymo_like.methodology_note: Held constant in v0.2 to keep the interface focused.

cybercab.methodology_note: No mature Cybercab mileage evidence exists; held equal to the Waymo-like case for comparability.

waymo_like.range_basis: Scenario range calibrated to the previous Waymo-like base model. Public operator fare data vary by city, trip and time, and do not provide a clean network-wide paid-mile average.
waymo_like.justification: Gross fare is separated from distribution leakage so the user can test direct-channel and aggregator-heavy models explicitly.
waymo_like.methodology_note: Passenger price before distribution or aggregator leakage. Taxes and operating costs shown elsewhere must not be deducted here.

cybercab.range_basis: Tesla's current introductory pricing is not evidence of mature Cybercab pricing. The range represents a lower-fare architecture sensitivity, not a forecast.
cybercab.justification: Tesla's stated strategy implies lower consumer pricing, but a stable commercial price per paid mile has not been demonstrated.
cybercab.methodology_note: Future Cybercab gross fare assumption. Do not treat the default as an observed Tesla network average.

waymo_like.range_basis: Official platform disclosures show variable service fees. Lyft reports an average fee near 14% and a 30% cap; Uber states its fee varies by trip. The base represents blended direct and partner distribution.
waymo_like.justification: A Waymo-like operator may sell directly and through partners such as ride-hailing aggregators. The leakage rate should therefore remain user-controlled.
waymo_like.methodology_note: Distribution or aggregator share only. Do not include taxes, tolls, insurance, city overhead or fleet operating cost, which belong elsewhere in the model.

cybercab.range_basis: Tesla currently distributes Robotaxi rides through its own app, supporting a direct-channel base. Future partner or third-party distribution remains possible.
cybercab.justification: A zero-leakage base tests the vertically integrated direct channel, while the full range allows the user to impose an aggregator or commercial distribution partner.
cybercab.methodology_note: Distribution or aggregator share only. A zero value does not mean zero payment, marketing, regulatory or support cost.

waymo_like.range_basis: Based on consolidated Waymo-like passenger-onboard versus total-mile evidence, with low and high cases reflecting fragmented versus dense operations.
waymo_like.justification: Every empty mile carries depreciation, energy, maintenance, insurance and operating cost.
waymo_like.methodology_note: Paid vehicle miles divided by total vehicle miles. It is not passenger occupancy and not vehicle availability.

cybercab.range_basis: Tesla has not disclosed a mature paid-mile ratio. The base reflects an early network with limited density; the high case represents a claims-realised mature system.
cybercab.justification: Tesla's cheaper vehicle does not eliminate deadheading. Utilisation remains a critical multiplier.
cybercab.methodology_note: Scenario input only. Current limited service is not a mature utilisation benchmark.

waymo_like.range_basis: Current-generation premium EV plus autonomous-integration scenario. Exact Waymo all-in vehicle cost is not disclosed.
waymo_like.justification: Vehicle capex affects depreciation, but the effect depends on lifetime miles and residual value.
waymo_like.methodology_note: Includes base vehicle and installed autonomous-driving hardware. Excludes depots and central platform development.

cybercab.range_basis: The $30,000 Cybercab figure is an aspirational endpoint. A higher evidence-weighted base recognises unproven production cost, commercial equipment and early ramp inefficiency.
cybercab.justification: Cybercab may be materially cheaper than a Waymo-like vehicle, but no audited bill of materials or realised fleet purchase price exists.
cybercab.methodology_note: The credible low is a claims-realised scenario, not a confirmed current cost. Includes vehicle and installed autonomy hardware.

waymo_like.range_basis: Commercial high-mileage scenario constrained by battery wear, accident exposure, interior deterioration and AV hardware obsolescence.
waymo_like.justification: Longer productive life spreads vehicle capital over more service.
waymo_like.methodology_note: Lifetime total miles before economic retirement or major replacement, not paid miles.

cybercab.range_basis: Cybercab commercial durability is unproven. Simplified design may help, while intensive use, wireless-charging duty and hardware generation changes may shorten economic life.
cybercab.justification: A low purchase price alone does not ensure low depreciation if useful life is short.
cybercab.methodology_note: No high-mileage Cybercab fleet evidence exists. Treat the full range as scenario-only.

waymo_like.range_basis: No mature secondary market exists for autonomous fleet vehicles. Potential value may come from batteries, components or a second commercial life inside the same autonomy ecosystem.
waymo_like.justification: Residual value materially changes depreciation and allows users to test scrap-only, component-recovery and second-life fleet cases.
waymo_like.methodology_note: Percentage of initial vehicle and AV-kit capex recovered at economic retirement. It is not necessarily an external retail resale value.

cybercab.range_basis: A two-seat vehicle without manual controls may have limited external resale value, although battery, components or continued use inside Tesla's network could retain value.
cybercab.justification: Cybercab residual value is especially uncertain because no secondary market or long-term fleet history exists.
cybercab.methodology_note: Percentage of initial vehicle cost recovered at economic retirement. The base is conservative and does not assume a consumer resale market.

waymo_like.range_basis: Consolidated scenario for energy, routine EV maintenance, AV-specific maintenance and insurance/liability reserve.
waymo_like.justification: This groups exposure-mile costs while keeping depreciation and local fleet operations separate.
waymo_like.methodology_note: Includes energy, routine maintenance, AV-specific maintenance and insurance/liability. Energy efficiency is folded into this variable to preserve the top-nine lever design.

cybercab.range_basis: Lower energy use and no LiDAR support a lower range, but insurance, repair, commercial durability and maintenance remain unverified.
cybercab.justification: Tesla's energy advantage affects this line, but it should not be confused with a validated total cost per mile.
cybercab.methodology_note: Includes energy, routine maintenance, autonomy-hardware maintenance and insurance/liability. Energy is not a separate slider because it is not among the top nine enterprise profitability levers.

waymo_like.range_basis: Scenario for depot readiness, charging infrastructure, remote assistance, rider support and field response after overlap controls.
waymo_like.justification: Driver removal shifts labour and infrastructure into depots, support centres and field operations.
waymo_like.methodology_note: Variable local cost per total mile. Excludes separate annual city cost.

cybercab.range_basis: Wireless charging and simpler sensors may reduce some tasks, but Tesla discloses no remote-assistance, cleaning, maintenance or field-response cost structure.
cybercab.justification: The conservative base avoids assuming that a cheaper vehicle produces human-free operations.
cybercab.methodology_note: Do not set to zero. Camera-only sensing and automated charging do not prove elimination of remote, cleaning or field labour.

waymo_like.range_basis: The 300 default is a triangulated network-average active-fleet-equivalent, based on Waymo's more than 4 million weekly miles, the model's 70,000 annual miles per active vehicle, and 11 current service markets. The 250–1,000 range is retained so users can test both low-density and highly concentrated city operations.
waymo_like.justification: The default represents vehicles generating the modelled annual mileage, not all 3,791 ADS-equipped units covered by the NHTSA recall population.
waymo_like.methodology_note: 300 vehicles per city is the default modelling proxy. It is not a disclosed city-by-city fleet allocation. The range remains deliberately broad for scenario exploration.

cybercab.range_basis: Tesla's current service is small and does not establish mature density. The high case represents successful scaled deployment.
cybercab.justification: Tesla manufacturing claims matter only if vehicles can be deployed densely enough to absorb local and platform costs.
cybercab.methodology_note: Average active commercial vehicles per city-equivalent. Current limited fleet counts are not used as a mature benchmark.

waymo_like.range_basis: Waymo's official rider page lists 11 current service markets. Waymo has also stated that it is laying groundwork for operations in more than 20 additional cities during 2026. The current default of 11 is high-confidence; the 25-city high case is a forward scenario.
waymo_like.justification: The current city count is observable and should not remain a generic six-city assumption. The forward range captures announced expansion while preserving uncertainty about launch timing and commercial maturity.
waymo_like.methodology_note: The default equals the 11 markets currently serving riders. Testing and announced markets are excluded from the default until they carry paying riders.

cybercab.range_basis: Limited current service does not establish a repeatable national launch process. The range tests Tesla's claimed replication advantage.
cybercab.justification: Tesla's map-light architecture may reduce one launch component, but regulation, support and operations remain local.
cybercab.methodology_note: Active commercial city-equivalents. The base intentionally reflects an early network.

waymo_like.range_basis: Transparent allocated scenario pool for core autonomy, AI production, simulation, data and software assurance. It is not derived from Alphabet Other Bets.
waymo_like.justification: The platform test is whether paid miles and validated coverage grow faster than the production burden.
waymo_like.methodology_note: Annual enterprise platform pool shared across all active vehicles and cities. Regional and city costs are excluded.

cybercab.range_basis: Tesla has large shared AI infrastructure, but no transparent robotaxi allocation. The higher base recognises that lower edge hardware may relocate cost into central AI and validation.
cybercab.justification: Consumer-FSD sharing may create leverage, while video training and validation remain substantial. Both effects are unquantified.
cybercab.methodology_note: Robotaxi-allocated annual platform pool. It is not Tesla's total AI spending and should not be set to zero because costs are shared.

waymo_like.range_basis: Combines recurring city overhead with annualised launch and ODD-validation cost. Public company-level launch economics are not disclosed.
waymo_like.justification: Each city reopens part of the cost stack. The key test is whether this burden falls across launch cohorts.
waymo_like.methodology_note: Includes recurring local overhead and annualised launch burden. Variable depot operations are excluded.

cybercab.range_basis: Map-light architecture supports a lower mapping component, but local validation, regulation, service support, charging and recovery remain.
cybercab.justification: Tesla may reduce, not eliminate, city-launch burden.
cybercab.methodology_note: Includes recurring local overhead and annualised launch burden. The lower base is an architectural scenario, not observed launch cost.

methodology_notes.architecture_vs_maturity: Architecture and maturity are separate dimensions because combining a Tesla vehicle-cost assumption with Waymo-shaped operating, platform and city-launch assumptions produces an incoherent hybrid. The architecture selector changes linked defaults, credible ranges, confidence and methodology; the maturity selector changes how developed that architecture has become.
methodology_notes.energy_in_direct_running_cost: Energy efficiency is folded into direct running cost rather than exposed as a separate slider because the public interface is limited to the nine highest-leverage variables. This is a prioritisation decision, not a claim that energy is unimportant. Architecture-specific energy evidence is reflected in the direct-running-cost defaults and ranges.
methodology_notes.waymo_city_and_fleet_basis: The Waymo-like default uses 11 current rider-service markets and 300 active-fleet-equivalent vehicles per city. The city count is directly observed. The vehicle-density default is triangulated from Waymo's reported weekly mileage and the model's annual-mileage assumption. The 250–1,000 range is retained for experimentation.
